channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK mortgage and housing finance news, sourced and rewritten daily.

item.title: UK house prices stall for second straight month as agents warn of summer slump
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK house prices remained stagnant for the second month running in June, with Nationwide's latest figures showing a marginal dip in average property values. The lender reported the typical home now costs £277,484, down slightly from £278,024 in May, as higher mortgage rates continue to influence buyer behaviour. This follows a 0.6% monthly decline recorded in May, contrasting with economists' expectations of modest growth.

Market sentiment shifts as rates bite

Estate agents report a noticeable change in market dynamics, with buyers becoming increasingly price-sensitive amidst elevated borrowing costs. While some families remain active in the market ahead of the new school year, transaction volumes appear more measured than in previous summers. Industry observers suggest many prospective purchasers are adopting a wait-and-see approach, particularly with ongoing economic uncertainty.

Mortgage rates have seen some recent easing following stabilisation in oil prices, yet remain significantly higher than pre-conflict levels. Moneyfacts data shows the average two-year fixed rate currently stands at 5.53%, up from 4.83% in early March. Five-year fixes follow a similar pattern, reflecting the broader trend of increased borrowing costs across the market.

Regional variations persist

Despite the flat monthly performance, annual price growth accelerated to 2.2% in June from 1.7% in May. Nationwide's quarterly analysis reveals all UK regions recorded positive annual growth, with Northern Ireland leading at 8.6%. Scotland and Wales both saw 3.5% increases, while London's market showed more modest growth of 1.6%.

Valuation professionals appear to be taking a cautious stance, with some reports of buyers negotiating aggressively on price. This more balanced market dynamic has impacted housebuilder shares, with several major developers seeing early Wednesday trading declines.

Looking ahead, some economists suggest potential relief may come if the Bank of England adopts a less aggressive stance on interest rates. With oil prices retreating from recent highs, there may be reduced pressure for further base rate increases. However, homeowners considering moves or remortgaging might wish to monitor developments closely, as the market appears set for a period of adjustment to the new interest rate environment.

item.title: What could US-Iran peace deal mean for UK household costs?
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Recent developments in US-Iran relations have sparked optimism in global markets, with the signing of a draft peace deal potentially easing tensions in the Gulf region. This move could have significant implications for energy prices worldwide, including in the UK. As oil and gas flows from the Gulf resume, households may see changes in their energy costs, though the situation remains fluid.

Impact on Energy Prices

The draft peace agreement has already influenced global oil prices, which have dropped below $80 a barrel after peaking above $126 during the height of the crisis. The easing of Iran’s blockade on the Strait of Hormuz, a critical trade route, has contributed to this decline. Similarly, European gas prices have fallen from over €61 per megawatt-hour to between €40 and €42/MWh. For UK households, this could translate into lower energy bills, particularly if the trend continues.

However, it is worth noting that the peace deal is not yet finalised, and recent disruptions to talks in Switzerland highlight the fragility of the situation. Borrowers and homeowners might wish to monitor these developments closely, as any further instability could reverse the current downward trend in energy prices.

Broader Economic Effects

Beyond energy costs, a lasting peace agreement could have wider economic implications. Reduced geopolitical tensions often lead to greater market stability, which may benefit UK consumers in various ways. For instance, lower oil prices could lead to reduced transportation costs, potentially lowering the price of goods and services across the board.

Additionally, a more stable global energy market could positively impact inflation rates. With energy being a significant component of household expenses, any sustained decrease in prices might ease the cost-of-living pressures many UK families are currently facing.

While the situation remains uncertain, homeowners may wish to consider how these potential changes could affect their finances. Keeping an eye on market trends and adjusting budgets accordingly could be a prudent approach in these evolving circumstances.

In summary, the draft US-Iran peace deal offers a glimpse of potential relief for UK households through lower energy costs and broader economic stability. However, with the deal still in its early stages and geopolitical tensions persisting, it remains essential to stay informed and prepared for any shifts in the global energy landscape.

item.title: US and UK central banks expected to keep interest rates on hold amid Iran peace deal
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Central banks on both sides of the Atlantic appear set to maintain current interest rate levels this week, with analysts suggesting recent geopolitical developments could help ease inflationary pressures. The Bank of England's Monetary Policy Committee and the US Federal Reserve are widely expected to hold rates steady at their respective meetings, following the announcement of a Middle East peace agreement that has already impacted global oil markets.

Bank of England's cautious stance

The Bank of England is anticipated to keep its base rate at 3.75% when policymakers convene this Thursday, despite UK inflation remaining above target at 2.8%. Economists suggest the MPC may adopt a "wait-and-see" approach following the Iran deal, which triggered an immediate softening of oil prices. Financial markets currently indicate expectations of one further rate rise this year, potentially in December.

James Smith, an economist at ING, noted: "Should the peace agreement hold and oil supplies stabilise, UK inflation might remain below 4%, potentially allowing the Bank to postpone any summer rate increases." This cautious outlook reflects uncertainty about both the durability of the geopolitical agreement and its longer-term impact on energy markets.

Global central bank responses

The US Federal Reserve, holding its first meeting under new chair Kevin Warsh, is expected to maintain rates between 3.5% and 3.75%. Market observers will scrutinise Warsh's post-meeting commentary for insights into the Fed's inflation outlook, particularly after US inflation reached 4.2% in May - a three-year high.

Meanwhile, the European Central Bank recently increased its main rate to 2.25% as eurozone inflation climbed to 3.2%. ECB President Christine Lagarde highlighted concerns about "second-round effects" of inflation, particularly regarding wage pressures. "When we observe these secondary inflationary risks emerging, particularly in wage growth, we must respond appropriately," Lagarde stated.

As central banks navigate these complex economic conditions, borrowers might consider reviewing their mortgage arrangements in light of potential future rate movements. With oil price volatility remaining a key factor, homeowners may wish to stay informed about how global events could influence domestic monetary policy in coming months.

item.title: UK house prices fall for first time this year amid rising mortgage rates
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK house prices experienced their first monthly decline this year in May, with Nationwide reporting a 0.6% drop compared to April. The average property now stands at £278,024, representing a modest 1.7% annual increase – a notable slowdown from April's 3% year-on-year growth. This cooling market follows several months of rising mortgage rates and economic uncertainty linked to global events.

Market slowdown defies seasonal expectations

Industry experts note the housing market typically gains momentum during spring, making this unexpected dip particularly significant. Robert Gardner, Nationwide's chief economist, suggests the "loss of momentum" reflects broader economic concerns, including Middle East tensions and their impact on energy prices and financial markets.

Mortgage rates have climbed steadily, with Moneyfacts data showing average two-year fixed deals reaching 5.68% and five-year fixes at 5.63% by May's end. Estate agents observe that properties agreed before recent rate rises are gradually completing, meaning the full effect of higher borrowing costs may not yet be fully reflected in market data.

Mixed forecasts for the year ahead

Property analysts offer differing views on the market's trajectory. Savills has revised its 2024 forecast from a 2% price increase to a 2% decrease, citing the changed economic landscape. However, Nationwide's Gardner points to swap rates remaining below 2023 peaks as a potential stabilising factor, suggesting any market softening could be temporary if economic conditions improve.

Several factors continue to influence the market:

  • Mortgage repayments consuming a historically high proportion of household incomes
  • Potential labour market weakening adding further pressure
  • Energy price fluctuations affecting consumer confidence

While the immediate outlook appears uncertain, borrowers might consider reviewing their mortgage options, particularly those approaching the end of fixed-term deals. Current market conditions highlight the importance of seeking professional financial advice to navigate this changing landscape.

item.title: ‘It feels unfair’: the Britons struggling to get a mortgage since Iran war began
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Recent global tensions have sent shockwaves through the UK mortgage market, with many prospective homeowners finding their borrowing costs rising unexpectedly. What began as a promising year for potential rate cuts has transformed into a challenging landscape for buyers, particularly first-time purchasers facing what some industry experts describe as the most difficult conditions since the 2008 financial crisis.

How global events impact local mortgages

The conflict in the Middle East has contributed to renewed inflationary pressures, prompting financial markets to revise their expectations for UK interest rates. Where many anticipated potential rate reductions in 2026, economists now suggest the Bank of England may need to implement at least one further rate hike this year. This shift has translated directly into higher mortgage costs, with some products seeing significant rate increases within weeks.

One London-based couple discovered their agreed mortgage rate had jumped from 4.18% to 5.22% between February and April, adding £500 to their monthly repayments. For many households, such increases can make the difference between an affordable purchase and having to postpone homeownership plans entirely. The situation highlights how interconnected global events and local housing markets have become in today's economy.

Navigating an uncertain market

Some homeowners who timed their property sales hoping for imminent rate cuts now find themselves in difficult positions. One Staffordshire family sold their home last October, expecting to benefit from falling borrowing costs when purchasing their next property. Instead, they've encountered both higher mortgage rates and limited housing stock, leaving them in rental accommodation with uncertain prospects.

For those affected, several strategies might help weather the current climate:

  • Reviewing mortgage product options with a whole-of-market broker
  • Considering longer fixed-rate terms for payment stability
  • Exploring alternative lenders or specialist products
  • Reassessing budget expectations and property criteria

While market conditions remain volatile, borrowers might find value in professional mortgage advice to understand their options. The current environment underscores the importance of building financial flexibility into homebuying plans, as global events can rapidly alter domestic borrowing landscapes. Many will be watching central bank decisions closely in coming months for signs of market stabilisation.

item.title: ‘Tracker mortgages are back’ – but is one the right choice for you?
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Tracker mortgages regain popularity amid economic uncertainty

With speculation growing about potential interest rate rises later this year, tracker mortgages are experiencing renewed interest from UK borrowers. These variable-rate deals, which move in line with the Bank of England base rate, are becoming increasingly competitive compared to fixed-rate alternatives. Mortgage brokers report a significant uptick in tracker applications, with some lenders seeing demand triple in recent months.

The shifting landscape follows economic turbulence linked to global conflicts, which has impacted swap rates used to price fixed mortgages. While fixed deals traditionally offered payment certainty at lower rates, the current market sees some trackers priced more competitively. Homeowners considering their options might find trackers particularly appealing if they anticipate base rate stability or potential decreases in the medium term.

Weighing up tracker versus fixed-rate mortgages

Current market conditions present borrowers with an unusual dilemma. At present, the cheapest two-year tracker rates hover around 3.96%, undercutting the most competitive fixed-rate deals at approximately 4.55%. This pricing inversion makes trackers worth considering for those comfortable with some interest rate uncertainty.

Several factors might influence this decision:

  • Flexibility - Many tracker deals don't carry early repayment charges
  • Potential savings - If base rates remain stable or fall, trackers could prove cheaper
  • Short-term strategy - Some borrowers use trackers as an interim solution while monitoring the market

However, financial advisers caution that trackers carry inherent risks. The Bank of England has signalled possible rate rises to combat inflation, with worst-case projections suggesting the base rate could reach 5.25% by 2027. Borrowers might wish to consider whether their household budget could accommodate such increases before committing to a variable-rate product.

Making the right mortgage choice

Mortgage selection ultimately depends on individual circumstances and risk appetite. Those prioritising payment certainty might still favour fixed-rate deals, despite their current premium. Conversely, borrowers comfortable with some variability could benefit from tracker rates while they remain competitive.

Industry experts suggest reviewing all options carefully, considering both current rates and potential future movements. Speaking with an independent mortgage adviser could help homeowners navigate these complex decisions, particularly during periods of economic uncertainty.

As the market continues to evolve, staying informed about rate trends and product availability remains crucial for anyone considering a new mortgage or remortgage in the current climate.

item.title: UK homebuyers face worst mortgage affordability since 2008, data shows
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK homeowners are experiencing the most significant mortgage affordability challenges since the 2008 financial crisis, according to recent data from UK Finance. The analysis reveals that initial mortgage repayments now account for over a fifth (21.3%) of a borrower’s gross income on average, marking the highest level in nearly two decades. However, the impact is not uniform across the country, with stark regional disparities influencing affordability.

Regional Disparities in Mortgage Affordability

The UK Finance report highlights considerable differences in affordability across the UK. Areas such as north Norfolk and Hillingdon in west London are among the least affordable, with homeowners dedicating more than a quarter of their income to mortgage repayments (25.7% and 25.1%, respectively). Other regions in the London commuter belt, including Luton, Slough, Broxbourne, and Harlow, also feature in the top 10 least affordable areas, with repayments consuming between 24% and 25% of gross income.

In contrast, Scotland dominates the list of the most affordable areas, with East Ayrshire and Inverclyde leading the way. Homebuyers in these regions typically spend around 17% of their income on mortgage repayments. Interestingly, the City of London ranks as the third most affordable area, a result attributed to the high-income brackets of those purchasing property in this expensive location. This anomaly underscores how local demographics and wage levels can significantly influence affordability metrics.

Economic Factors Impacting Affordability

The data, which focuses on 2025, does not account for recent economic developments, such as the impact of global conflicts on mortgage rates. These events have contributed to rising borrowing costs, leaving many homeowners facing higher repayments than anticipated. Whilst the report provides a snapshot of affordability pressures, the evolving economic landscape could further exacerbate challenges for prospective buyers.

James Tatch, head of analytics at UK Finance, emphasised that the past few years have been particularly tough for those looking to purchase property. He noted that affordability pressures vary widely across the UK, driven by differences in property prices, wages, and local demographics. This uneven distribution means that some regions are feeling the strain far more acutely than others.

For homeowners navigating these challenges, exploring options such as extending mortgage terms or seeking fixed-rate deals might help mitigate rising costs. However, it is essential to carefully consider individual financial circumstances and seek professional advice where necessary.

As mortgage affordability continues to tighten, understanding regional variations and economic trends could prove crucial for making informed decisions in today’s housing market.

item.title: How much worse could Iran war make the cost of living crisis? – The Latest
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The ongoing conflict in the Middle East has sparked concerns about its potential impact on the UK’s cost of living crisis. With rising oil prices and market instability, households could face increased financial pressures, particularly in areas such as energy bills, mortgage rates, and petrol costs. As the situation unfolds, homeowners and borrowers may wish to stay informed about how these global events could affect their personal finances.

Energy Bills and Oil Prices

One of the most immediate effects of the conflict could be felt in energy costs. The Middle East is a significant global supplier of oil, and any disruption to production or supply chains can lead to higher prices. For UK households, this could translate into steeper energy bills, particularly as winter approaches. Those on variable energy tariffs might see their costs rise more sharply, while fixed-rate customers could face higher prices when their contracts end. It could be worth reviewing energy plans and considering ways to reduce consumption to mitigate potential increases.

Mortgage Rates and Market Volatility

The conflict has also introduced uncertainty into global financial markets, which could influence UK mortgage rates. Lenders often respond to market instability by raising interest rates to offset risk. Borrowers with tracker or variable-rate mortgages might see their repayments increase, while those looking to secure a new mortgage could face higher rates. Homeowners may wish to explore their options, such as remortgaging to a fixed-rate deal, to lock in more predictable payments. Consulting a mortgage adviser could provide clarity on the best course of action in a volatile market.

Petrol Prices and Everyday Costs

Rising oil prices are also likely to impact the cost of petrol and diesel, which could increase everyday expenses for drivers. Higher fuel costs can have a knock-on effect on the prices of goods and services, as transportation becomes more expensive. This could further strain household budgets, particularly for those who rely on their cars for work or essential travel. Drivers might consider ways to reduce fuel consumption, such as carpooling or using public transport where possible, to manage these rising costs.

While the full extent of the conflict’s impact on the UK economy remains uncertain, staying informed and proactive could help households navigate potential financial challenges. Monitoring energy usage, reviewing mortgage options, and finding ways to reduce everyday expenses are steps that may provide some stability during this uncertain time.

Consulting a mortgage adviser could provide clarity on the best course of action in a volatile market.

Petrol Prices and Everyday Costs

Rising oil prices are also likely to impact the cost of petrol and diesel, which could increase everyday expenses for drivers. Higher fuel costs can have a knock-on effect on the prices of goods and services, as transportation becomes more expensive. This could further strain household budgets, particularly for those who rely on their cars for work or essential travel. Drivers might consider ways to reduce fuel consumption, such as carpooling or using public transport where possible, to manage these rising costs.

While the full extent of the conflict’s impact on the UK economy remains uncertain, staying informed and proactive could help households navigate potential financial challenges. Monitoring energy usage, reviewing mortgage options, and finding ways to reduce everyday expenses are steps that may provide some stability during this uncertain time.

item.title: Average UK mortgage rate tops 5% as lenders scurry to reprice loans amid Middle East crisis
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK mortgage market has seen significant turbulence in recent days, with average fixed rates climbing above 5% as lenders adjust pricing in response to global economic uncertainty. This marks the sharpest rise in borrowing costs since the aftermath of the 2022 mini-budget, though industry analysts note the current situation differs in scale from that period.

Mortgage rates climb amid market volatility

Data from Moneyfacts reveals the average two-year fixed mortgage rate reached 5.01% this week, up from 4.84% before recent geopolitical tensions escalated. Five-year fixed deals now average 5.09%, returning to levels last seen in summer 2023. Major lenders including HSBC, Nationwide, Halifax and Barclays have repriced products, with nearly 500 mortgage deals withdrawn from the market in just 48 hours.

Adam French of Moneyfacts commented: "While we're seeing considerable movement in the market, the current product withdrawals remain below the extreme volatility witnessed in late 2022 when nearly 1,000 deals disappeared in a single day." The changes come as swap rates - which lenders use to price fixed mortgages - have risen sharply due to shifting expectations about future Bank of England base rate decisions.

Global factors influencing UK mortgage costs

The recent rate increases reflect broader economic concerns rather than domestic policy changes. Before recent geopolitical developments, markets had anticipated potential base rate cuts later this year. However, the prospect of prolonged higher energy prices and inflationary pressures has led to revised expectations, with the likelihood of a 2024 rate reduction now estimated at just 20% compared to 80% previously.

This shift presents challenges for both prospective homebuyers and those approaching the end of fixed-rate deals. With approximately 1.8 million fixed-rate mortgages due to expire in 2026, many homeowners may wish to review their options earlier than planned. Financial advisers typically recommend beginning remortgage research 4-6 months before a current deal ends.

While the current market movement creates uncertainty, borrowers might consider speaking with a whole-of-market mortgage broker to understand how these changes could affect their individual circumstances. As always, keeping abreast of market developments while focusing on long-term affordability remains crucial when making mortgage decisions.

item.title: How will war in the Middle East affect your finances?
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The ongoing conflict in the Middle East has created ripples across global financial markets, with potential implications for UK homeowners and savers. As oil prices climb and stock markets react, many households may be wondering how these developments could influence their mortgage rates, living costs and long-term financial planning.

Potential impact on mortgage rates

Geopolitical instability often leads to market volatility, which can indirectly affect UK mortgage pricing. The Bank of England's Monetary Policy Committee considers various economic indicators when setting the base rate, including:

  • Global energy prices and their effect on inflation
  • Investor sentiment in bond markets
  • Wider economic growth projections

With oil prices surpassing $100 per barrel, borrowers might see sustained pressure on inflation figures. This could influence the central bank's decisions on future base rate adjustments, potentially affecting variable rate mortgage holders. Those approaching the end of fixed-term deals may wish to monitor the situation closely when considering their remortgage options.

Broader financial considerations

The conflict's economic consequences extend beyond the housing market, with several areas that could impact personal finances:

  • Energy bills: Higher oil prices may translate to increased costs for heating and transportation
  • Pension funds: Market volatility could affect the performance of investment portfolios
  • Savings rates: Continued inflationary pressures might influence the real value of cash savings

Whilst the situation remains fluid, maintaining a diversified financial strategy could help mitigate some of these risks. Homeowners with significant savings might consider reviewing their emergency funds to account for potential cost increases.

As global events continue to unfold, staying informed about economic developments may help individuals make more confident financial decisions. Those with specific concerns about their mortgage or savings may find it beneficial to consult an independent financial adviser for personalised guidance.

item.title: UK interest rate cuts unlikely this year amid Iran war – and a rise could be ahead
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Financial markets have dramatically shifted their expectations for UK interest rates following escalating tensions in the Middle East, with analysts now suggesting rate cuts appear unlikely this year and a potential increase could be on the horizon in 2025. The Bank of England's base rate, currently at 3.75%, may remain unchanged throughout 2024 before rising to 4% next summer, according to the latest market predictions.

How global conflicts are influencing UK rates

The geopolitical situation has caused significant volatility in financial markets, with the conflict between Israel and Iran creating uncertainty about global oil supplies. Approximately 20% of the world's oil passes through the Strait of Hormuz, which has effectively become closed due to the hostilities. This development initially pushed Brent crude prices to $119 per barrel before settling at $104 after G7 finance ministers announced emergency talks.

Market indicators show a 99% probability that the Bank of England will maintain current rates at its next meeting on 19th March, a stark contrast to the 80% chance of a cut predicted before the conflict began. UK two-year bond yields, often seen as an indicator of future interest rates, have risen sharply to 4.129% - their highest level since April 2025 and the most significant daily increase since the market turbulence following Liz Truss's 2022 mini-budget.

What this means for UK mortgages

The changing interest rate outlook is already having an impact on the mortgage market. Recent data shows the average two-year fixed residential mortgage rate has increased to 4.87%, up from 4.84% last week, while five-year fixes have risen to 4.98% from 4.96%. These increases come as lenders adjust their pricing in response to the shifting economic landscape.

Homeowners approaching the end of their fixed-rate deals might consider reviewing their options earlier than planned, as the window for securing competitive rates could be narrowing. Those currently on variable rate mortgages may wish to explore whether fixing could provide more payment stability during this period of uncertainty.

Meanwhile, stock markets have experienced significant volatility, with the FTSE 100 initially dropping 1.9% before recovering some ground. Similar patterns were seen across European markets, reflecting investor concerns about prolonged disruption to global energy supplies and its potential inflationary effects.

As the situation develops, borrowers would be wise to stay informed about market movements and consider seeking independent financial advice to understand how these changes might affect their personal circumstances. The Bank of England's future decisions will likely depend on how the geopolitical situation evolves and its impact on inflation in the coming months.

item.title: ‘Geopolitical uncertainties’ amid Iran war could slow fall in mortgage rates, says Halifax
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Recent geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, particularly involving Iran, could influence the trajectory of mortgage rates in the UK, according to Halifax. The lender, part of Lloyds Banking Group, has highlighted that escalating conflicts may impact global economies, fuel inflation, and potentially delay anticipated interest rate cuts. This, in turn, could slow the expected decline in mortgage rates, affecting borrowing costs for homebuyers.

House Price Growth Slows in February

Halifax’s latest data reveals that UK house prices rose by 0.3% in February, bringing the average property value to £301,151. Whilst this marks a modest increase, it represents a notable slowdown compared to January’s 0.8% growth, which pushed average prices above £300,000 for the first time. The easing in house price growth suggests a cooling market, albeit against a backdrop of broader economic uncertainties.

Amanda Bryden, head of mortgages at Halifax, commented, “Geopolitical uncertainties appear poised to shape the outlook for inflation and the wider economy. In this context, markets are now anticipating a more gradual path for interest rate reductions. If this materialises, the pace at which borrowing costs ease could be tempered.”

Impact of Geopolitical Tensions on Mortgage Rates

The conflict in the Middle East has already influenced financial markets, with rising energy prices and reduced expectations for central bank rate cuts. Swap rates, which underpin the pricing of fixed-rate mortgages, have edged higher amid concerns that inflationary pressures could persist. This has led several lenders, including HSBC, Nationwide, and Coventry Building Society, to increase rates on their fixed mortgage deals.

Mark Harris, chief executive of mortgage broker SPF Private Clients, noted, “The situation in the Middle East has lifted energy prices and dampened expectations for imminent rate cuts. As swap rates have risen, lenders have adjusted their mortgage rates accordingly. The likelihood of a base rate cut in the near term has diminished significantly.”

Market Sentiment and Buyer Behaviour

The uncertainty surrounding interest rates and inflation has also impacted buyer behaviour. Jeremy Leaf, a north London estate agent and former chair of the Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors, observed, “Since the conflict began, some buyers and sellers have paused their plans. If uncertainties persist beyond the short term, this hesitation could intensify.”

Despite the slowdown in February, the housing market had shown signs of steady recovery earlier in the year. However, the current geopolitical climate introduces a new layer of unpredictability, which could shape market dynamics in the coming months.

For homeowners and prospective buyers, staying informed about market trends and seeking professional advice could help navigate these uncertain times. Whilst mortgage rates may not fall as quickly as previously anticipated, understanding the broader economic context remains crucial for making informed decisions.

item.title: UK mortgage interest rates expected to rise despite Trump’s Iran pause
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Despite a temporary easing of geopolitical tensions following US President Donald Trump’s decision to delay airstrikes on Iran, UK mortgage rates are still expected to rise. This comes as financial markets anticipate further inflationary pressures, prompting lenders to adjust their offerings. Homeowners may find their options shrinking, with hundreds of mortgage products already withdrawn from the market.

Market Reactions and Mortgage Rate Trends

Earlier this week, financial markets reacted to Trump’s pause on military action against Iran, initially reducing expectations of Bank of England rate rises from four to two quarter-point increases this year. However, concerns over sustained inflation due to global instability have kept pressure on mortgage rates. The average two-year fixed residential mortgage rate has climbed to 5.43%, the highest since February 2025, up from 4.83% at the start of March.

Mortgage brokers and analysts have noted that lenders are rapidly repricing their products to keep pace with market expectations. Nicholas Mendes, a mortgage adviser at John Charcol, highlighted that lenders are not waiting for the Bank of England to act. Instead, they are adjusting rates in advance based on market forecasts, leading to further upward pressure on fixed-rate mortgages.

Impact on Homeowners and the Mortgage Market

The uncertainty surrounding inflation and interest rates has had a significant impact on the mortgage market. According to Moneyfacts, the number of residential mortgage products available has dropped from 6,659 to 6,144 in just a few days. This reduction in choice, coupled with rising rates, could make it more challenging for borrowers to secure favourable deals.

While some analysts, including Bank of England governor Andrew Bailey, have suggested that markets may be overestimating the likelihood of multiple rate rises this year, lenders remain cautious. The potential for inflation to exceed 3% due to geopolitical events has kept the market on edge, with borrowers likely to face higher costs in the coming months.

For homeowners, this environment underscores the importance of reviewing their mortgage options promptly. Those considering remortgaging or securing a new deal might find it beneficial to act sooner rather than later, as further rate increases could be on the horizon.

As the situation evolves, keeping a close eye on market trends and seeking advice from mortgage brokers could help borrowers navigate these uncertain times.

item.title: Why are mortgage rates going up when the Bank of England base rate hasn’t changed?
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Mortgage rates have been climbing steadily in early 2024 despite the Bank of England maintaining its base rate at 3.75%. This counterintuitive trend has left many borrowers wondering why their home loan costs are increasing when the central bank's key rate remains unchanged. The answer lies in global economic pressures, market expectations and the complex mechanisms behind fixed-rate mortgage pricing.

Why fixed mortgage rates don't always follow the base rate

Fixed-rate mortgages operate differently from variable-rate products. Whilst tracker mortgages move directly in line with Bank of England decisions, fixed rates are influenced by longer-term financial market expectations. Lenders typically fund these products through a combination of retail deposits and wholesale money markets, with swap rates playing a crucial role in pricing.

Financial data from Moneyfacts shows the average two-year fixed rate rose from 4.78% in mid-January to 5.20% by mid-March. This increase occurred despite no change in the base rate during this period. Market analysts point to several contributing factors:

  • Geopolitical tensions affecting global markets
  • Rising oil and commodity prices
  • Increased inflation expectations
  • Changing forecasts for future base rate movements

How swap rates influence mortgage pricing

The interest rate swap market allows lenders to manage the risk associated with offering fixed-rate mortgages. In simple terms, financial institutions use swaps to exchange variable-rate obligations for fixed-rate ones, effectively hedging against future rate fluctuations.

Adam French of Moneyfacts explains: "Lenders balance their funding between customer deposits and wholesale borrowing. When market expectations shift – perhaps due to global events or economic forecasts – the swap rates that underpin fixed mortgage deals adjust accordingly." This mechanism explains why fixed mortgage rates can rise even when the base rate stays static.

Wealth management experts note that these financial instruments create a fair risk distribution between parties. Those preferring certainty opt for fixed rates, whilst others might benefit from variable products if rates fall. The swap market essentially facilitates this balance between different risk appetites.

For homeowners considering their options, it's worth remembering that mortgage pricing reflects numerous factors beyond the base rate. Global events, market sentiment and long-term economic projections all play their part in determining the fixed-rate deals available to borrowers.

item.title: New mortgages up by £800 a year amid ‘Trumpflation’ from Iran war
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Mortgage Rates Rise Sharply Amid Global Uncertainty

Homeowners seeking new fixed-rate mortgages may face significantly higher costs as global economic instability drives up borrowing rates. Recent data from Moneyfacts reveals the average two-year fixed mortgage rate has climbed from 4.83% to 5.28% in just two weeks – the highest level since April 2025. Similarly, five-year fixes have risen from 4.95% to 5.32%, marking the steepest increase since February 2025.

For those with a £250,000 mortgage over 25 years, these shifts could mean paying an additional £788 annually on a two-year fix or £651 more on a five-year deal compared to early March. The surge follows geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and subsequent market volatility, with nearly 700 mortgage products withdrawn by lenders in recent days.

Economic Fallout Impacts Borrowers

The rapid repricing of mortgages stems from rising swap rates, which lenders use to determine fixed-rate pricing. Financial markets now anticipate the Bank of England will maintain its base rate at 3.75% this week, abandoning earlier predictions of cuts. Some analysts suggest further rate hikes could follow if inflationary pressures from elevated oil prices persist.

Mortgage availability has contracted dramatically, with only nine sub-4% deals remaining nationwide – down from 490 last week. Adam French of Moneyfacts cautions: "Funding costs continue to climb unpredictably, leaving borrowers facing dwindling options and potential further turbulence."

Approximately 1.8 million fixed-rate mortgages are scheduled to expire in 2026, meaning many homeowners might need to refinance at notably higher rates. Whilst the full economic impact remains uncertain, prospective buyers and those remortgaging may wish to seek professional advice to navigate this volatile period.

item.title: High levels of debt on essential UK bills are the ‘new normal’, warn campaigners
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Many households across the UK are finding themselves trapped in a cycle of debt, with essential bills becoming increasingly difficult to manage. According to recent data from the debt charity StepChange, rising arrears on housing, utilities, and council tax have become the "new normal" for low-income families. This trend highlights the ongoing financial pressures faced by many, despite some easing in mortgage and rent costs.

Growing Arrears on Housing and Energy Bills

The figures reveal a concerning rise in arrears across key household expenses. Average rent arrears increased by 15% to £2,372, whilst mortgage arrears saw a sharper jump of 22%, rising from £10,239 in 2024 to £12,534 in 2025. Energy bills, although lower than their 2022 peak, continue to pose a significant burden. Over a third of StepChange's clients were in debt to energy companies, with the average debt growing by £220 to £2,560. This suggests that whilst energy prices have stabilised, many households are still struggling to catch up on missed payments.

The charity also noted that two in five of its clients were receiving Universal Credit, and three in five lived in rented accommodation. These figures underline the financial vulnerability of many households, particularly those relying on social support or living in the private rental sector.

Calls for Government Intervention

StepChange has urged the government to take further action to alleviate the financial strain on low-income families. The charity is advocating for the introduction of national social tariffs for energy and water, which could help bring costs down to a more manageable level for those on tight budgets. Vikki Brownridge, Chief Executive of StepChange, emphasised that "the cost of everyday essentials remains prohibitively high for many households," and the current pressures show no signs of easing.

Brownridge added that rising arrears across housing, energy, and consumer credit debt have become a persistent issue, reflecting the broader economic challenges faced by households. The charity's call for social tariffs aims to provide a safety net for those with low incomes or high needs, helping to prevent them from falling into deeper debt just to cover essential costs.

As household budgets continue to be stretched by rising prices and economic uncertainty, the need for targeted support has never been more apparent. Borrowers and renters facing financial difficulties might consider seeking advice from debt charities or exploring available government schemes to manage their arrears more effectively.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Many households across the UK are finding themselves trapped in a cycle of debt, with essential bills becoming increasingly difficult to manage. According to recent data from the debt charity StepChange, rising arrears on housing, utilities, and council tax have become the "new normal" for low-income families. This trend highlights the ongoing financial pressures faced by many, despite some easing in mortgage and rent costs.

Growing Arrears on Housing and Energy Bills

The figures reveal a concerning rise in arrears across key household expenses. Average rent arrears increased by 15% to £2,372, whilst mortgage arrears saw a sharper jump of 22%, rising from £10,239 in 2024 to £12,534 in 2025. Energy bills, although lower than their 2022 peak, continue to pose a significant burden. Over a third of StepChange's clients were in debt to energy companies, with the average debt growing by £220 to £2,560. This suggests that whilst energy prices have stabilised, many households are still struggling to catch up on missed payments.

The charity also noted that two in five of its clients were receiving Universal Credit, and three in five lived in rented accommodation. These figures underline the financial vulnerability of many households, particularly those relying on social support or living in the private rental sector.

Calls for Government Intervention

StepChange has urged the government to take further action to alleviate the financial strain on low-income families. The charity is advocating for the introduction of national social tariffs for energy and water, which could help bring costs down to a more manageable level for those on tight budgets. Vikki Brownridge, Chief Executive of StepChange, emphasised that "the cost of everyday essentials remains prohibitively high for many households," and the current pressures show no signs of easing.

Brownridge added that rising arrears across housing, energy, and consumer credit debt have become a persistent issue, reflecting the broader economic challenges faced by households. The charity's call for social tariffs aims to provide a safety net for those with low incomes or high needs, helping to prevent them from falling into deeper debt just to cover essential costs.

As household budgets continue to be stretched by rising prices and economic uncertainty, the need for targeted support has never been more apparent. Borrowers and renters facing financial difficulties might consider seeking advice from debt charities or exploring available government schemes to manage their arrears more effectively.

item.title: UK housing costs rise 41% over five years for renters and owners, study shows
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

UK households spent a record £226 billion on housing costs in 2023, marking a significant increase over recent years. According to a study by property group Savills, housing expenses have risen by 41% over the past five years, with mortgage interest payments contributing more than half of this surge. This trend highlights the growing financial pressure on homeowners and renters alike, particularly those coming to the end of fixed-rate mortgage deals.

Mortgage Costs Drive Housing Expense Surge

Mortgage interest payments saw a sharp 9% rise last year, reaching £53.6 billion. This increase accounted for more than half of the overall £8 billion growth in housing costs in 2023. With many homeowners facing higher rates after their fixed-term deals expire, the burden of rising mortgage payments has become a significant concern. Savills noted that if inflation persists due to global economic uncertainties, this trend could continue, further impacting household budgets.

The average two-year fixed-rate mortgage recently surpassed 5%, up from 4.84% at the end of February. Lenders have been adjusting their offerings, pulling some deals and raising rates in response to market conditions. For the 8.8 million mortgage holders in the UK, this has translated to an average annual payment of £13,000, with total mortgage costs reaching £114 billion in 2023.

Rental Market Trends and Regional Variations

While the rental market experienced slower growth, costs still rose by 2.75% to £112 billion in 2023. Private sector landlords received £81 billion, averaging £15,000 per property. Over the past five years, private renters have seen a 27% increase in their housing expenses, reflecting the broader upward trend in the housing market.

Regional disparities also play a role in housing cost increases. London recorded the smallest percentage rise at 36%, compared to 49% in the north-west and 45% in the north-east and eastern England. Despite this, London remains the largest contributor to the UK’s housing costs, accounting for 23.4% of the total.

As the housing market continues to evolve, homeowners and renters may wish to explore their options carefully. Monitoring mortgage deals and seeking professional advice could help mitigate the impact of rising costs in the years ahead.

item.title: Have you lost a UK mortgage deal or seen your mortage rate increase? We would like to speak to you
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The ongoing conflict in the Middle East has had ripple effects far beyond the region, influencing global markets and consumer confidence. In the UK, one of the most notable impacts has been on the mortgage market. Amid concerns over rising oil prices and inflationary pressures, lenders swiftly withdrew hundreds of mortgage products within 48 hours of the conflict’s escalation. These products were often replaced with deals carrying higher interest rates, leaving many borrowers facing increased costs or losing previously secured offers.

The Impact on Mortgage Rates and House Prices

The sudden shift in the mortgage market has coincided with a decline in average UK house prices. According to data from Halifax, prices fell by 0.5% in March, reflecting reduced demand as higher mortgage rates began to take their toll. For prospective buyers, this could mean having to reassess their budgets or delaying plans altogether. Existing homeowners looking to remortgage may also find themselves facing steeper repayments than anticipated.

For those who had secured mortgage deals before the conflict, the situation has been particularly challenging. Many lenders withdrew these offers, leaving borrowers to navigate a market with fewer options and higher costs. This has forced some to reconsider their financial plans, whether that involves postponing a house purchase, exploring alternative mortgage products, or seeking advice from financial experts.

Coping with Changes in the Mortgage Market

Homeowners and buyers affected by these changes may wish to take proactive steps to manage their finances. One option could be to consult a mortgage broker, who can provide tailored advice and help identify suitable products in the current market. Additionally, borrowers might consider reviewing their budgets to account for potential increases in monthly repayments.

For those who have lost a mortgage deal, it could be worth exploring whether any alternative offers are available from their lender or other providers. Whilst rates may be higher than before, shopping around could still uncover deals that align with individual financial circumstances. It’s also important to stay informed about market trends, as fluctuations in oil prices and inflation could continue to influence mortgage rates in the coming months.

Ultimately, the current uncertainty highlights the importance of careful financial planning. Whether you’re a first-time buyer, looking to remortgage, or simply keeping an eye on the market, staying informed and seeking professional guidance could help navigate these challenging times.

item.title: UK house prices fall in March amid uncertain impact of Middle East conflict
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

UK house prices experienced a slight decline in March, with the average property price dipping below the £300,000 mark to £299,677, according to data from Halifax, part of Lloyds Banking Group. This marks a 0.5% monthly decrease and a slowdown in annual price growth to 0.8%, down from 1.2% in February. The drop comes at a time when the housing market faces uncertainty, influenced by global events and shifting economic expectations.

Global Events and Economic Pressures

The ongoing conflict in the Middle East has been cited as a contributing factor to the market’s subdued performance. Halifax noted that this uncertainty has dampened the initial momentum seen at the start of the year. Rising energy prices and inflation expectations have also played a role, pushing mortgage rates higher. The Bank of England’s potential interest rate hikes have further driven up the cost of fixed-rate mortgages, adding to the challenges for prospective buyers.

However, recent developments, such as the temporary ceasefire agreement between the US and Iran, have led to revised forecasts. City traders now anticipate just one quarter-point interest rate rise this year, down from earlier predictions of multiple increases. This shift could provide some relief to borrowers, though the overall impact on the housing market remains uncertain.

Mortgage Rates and Market Dynamics

The average two-year fixed residential mortgage rate climbed to 5.84% by the end of March, the highest level since July 2024. This increase has been accompanied by a reduction in the availability of mortgage products, with hundreds of deals withdrawn from the market in recent weeks. Amanda Bryden, Head of Mortgages at Halifax, highlighted that mortgage rates are a critical factor for buyers, particularly first-time homeowners who are already navigating the dual challenges of saving for a deposit and managing borrowing costs.

Despite the rise in mortgage rates, the increase has been more moderate compared to the sharp spikes seen following the 2022 mini-budget under Liz Truss. Many households remain shielded from immediate rate hikes due to existing fixed-rate mortgage deals. However, those looking to secure new mortgages or remortgage may face higher costs in the coming months.

Outlook for Homebuyers

Prospective buyers are likely to monitor mortgage rates closely before committing to a purchase. The interplay between global events, inflation, and interest rate decisions will continue to shape the housing market in the months ahead. Halifax’s Bryden emphasised that the duration of these economic pressures, as well as their broader implications for unemployment and consumer confidence, will be key factors influencing house prices.

For those considering entering the market, it could be worth exploring available mortgage deals and seeking advice to understand how rising rates might affect affordability. Whilst the market faces headwinds, opportunities may still arise for buyers who are well-prepared and informed.

As the spring selling season progresses, the housing market’s trajectory will depend on a range of factors, from geopolitical developments to domestic economic conditions. Homeowners and buyers alike may wish to stay attuned to market trends and seek professional guidance to navigate this evolving landscape.

item.title: UK interest rate predictions fall as US and Iran agree two-week ceasefire
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Mortgage Rate Expectations Shift as Geopolitical Tensions Ease

Financial markets have significantly revised their predictions for UK interest rate rises following news of a temporary ceasefire between the US and Iran. Where traders previously anticipated multiple rate increases this year, current pricing suggests only one potential rise by December, which would lift the Bank of England base rate to 4%. This marks a notable shift from earlier forecasts that suggested more aggressive monetary tightening.

The changing outlook comes as oil prices fell sharply following the diplomatic breakthrough, with Brent crude dropping to $94.71 per barrel from $109 before the ceasefire announcement. This development has eased some inflationary pressures that had been driving rate expectations upwards throughout the conflict period.

What This Means for UK Borrowers

The recalibration of rate expectations has begun influencing mortgage markets, though homeowners might not see immediate relief. According to Moneyfacts data, the average two-year fixed-rate mortgage has climbed from 4.83% at the beginning of March to 5.90% - the highest level since July 2024.

Market analysts suggest that while swap rates have started declining in response to the improved geopolitical situation, mortgage rates may remain elevated in the short term. Chris Beauchamp of IG noted: "The ceasefire news brings relief across several fronts, including potential reductions in petrol prices. However, the dramatic rate cut expectations we saw earlier this year appear unlikely to return soon."

For those considering their mortgage options, it could be worth noting that current market pricing indicates approximately 32 basis points of rate increases expected this year, down from 62 basis points before the ceasefire. This suggests that while the pace of rate rises may slow, borrowers might still face higher costs compared to the start of the year.

As the situation remains fluid, homeowners may wish to stay informed about market developments and consult independent mortgage advisers to understand how these changes could affect their individual circumstances. While the immediate pressure on rates appears to be easing, volatility in global markets could still influence the UK's mortgage landscape in coming months.
